text: Laparoscopic Hartman's reversal after emergent laparoscopic Hartman's procedure. Tips for dealing with a difficult rectal stump. The use of minimally invasive surgery for emergent and elective conditions continues to expand and evolve. Multiple studies have documented the feasibility of a laparoscopic Hartman's procedure for complicated diverticulitis that is not amenable to an anastomosis with proximal diversion. Others have also shown the laparoscopic approach to Hartman's reversal while challenging, is feasible and associated with better outcomes than the open approach. In theory, a Hartman's procedure performed laparoscopically should make the subsequent reversal more technically feasible. Our case is that of a forty-nine-year-old female with no past medical history who initially presented to our emergency department with HENCHU-III diverticulitis. She underwent an emergent laparoscopic Hartman's procedure. Of note, the perforation was in the proximal sigmoid with a significant amount of normal and uninvolved distal colon and rectum. The decision was made to leave a portion of the sigmoid in place in the hopes of facilitating an easier laparoscopic reversal. Six months postoperatively, she underwent a colonoscopy and flexible proctoscopy, which was significant for a long rectal stump of approximately twenty centimeters, and this of course included some sigmoid colon. In this video, we present our approach to the laparoscopic colostomy reversal, which illustrates that despite an initial laparoscopic approach, the rectal dissection can still be quite challenging. We use a four-port technique, a Hassan, and a XII for the stapler.

The video shows the initial laparoscopic Hartmann's procedure for HINCI-III diverticulitis, with visible purulence in the pelvis extending into the cul-de-sac and a perforation in the proximal sigmoid colon. The mesentery appears thickened, and the remaining sigmoid colon is soft and compliant. During the reversal operation, the appendix is tethered to the adnexate, with few adhesions in the pelvis. The surgeon begins dissection at the cul-de-sac using Metzenbaum scissors, noting significant inflammation. The remnant sigmoid colon and its mesentery are visible, with the surgeon switching to a bipolar sealing device for dissection, aiming to preserve the superior rectal artery.

The surgeon observes that posteriorly, the rectum and colon appear supple, while anteriorly there is significant thickening and inflammation. The bowel looks unhealthy, and the circumferential dissection of the rectum is incomplete, making it difficult to identify the bowel wall. The thickened peritoneum at the cul-de-sac creates tight angulations, complicating stapler placement. The surgeon cleans the pararectal fat to straighten the rectum and uses a small dilator to address a stricture in the proximal rectum. After dividing, the splenic flexure is mobilized, and the omentum is taken off the transverse colon. The descending colon is mobilized, and the anastomosis is performed intracorporeally. The stapler is positioned correctly, and an air leak test confirms no leaks, with two robust donuts and an intact anastomosis observed via flexible proctoscopy.

Our operative time was approximately ten or ninety-seven minutes. Her post-operative course was uneventful. She had resumption of bowel function on post-operative day two and was discharged to home on post-operative day three. Here you see the pre-op photo in the operating room after the first operation, and this is a six-week post-op view of her abdomen. While the minimally invasive approach to colostomy reversals are feasible, significant challenges remain. In our series of approximately ten patients, all required dissection and division of the rectum to facilitate a safe anastomosis. An initial laparoscopic approach for Hartman's procedure has the advantage of limiting significant small bowel adhesions. However, dissection of the rectal stump can still be challenging. Our video illustrates the difficulties encountered during a laparoscopic Hartman's reversal and offers strategic tips to ensure success with a difficult procedure. Future studies may want to focus on factors to help predict a difficult rectal stump. Never mind making the minimally invasive approach to the lost mu reversal safer and more accessible. Thank you.
